title: 529 Plans - Qualified Tuition Programs
description: Understand 529 plan types, contribution rules, tax benefits, and qualified expenses for education savings

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: 529 Plans are state-sponsored, tax-advantaged savings accounts designed to encourage saving for future education costs. Understanding the different types, contribution rules, and qualified expenses is essential for CFP® professionals advising families on education planning strategies.

sections.title: Two Types of 529 Plans
sections.content: There are two main types of 529 plans, each with different features and benefits.

sections.title: 529 College Savings Plans
rows: Cash only, invested in mutual fund-like options
Account owner controls investments and distributions
Tax-deferred; tax-free if used for qualified expenses

sections.title: Prepaid Tuition Plans
rows: Covers future tuition regardless of increases
Usually limited to participating schools (often in-state)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Tip: 529 Plan Types
sections.content: Most exam questions focus on 529 College Savings Plans. Know both types exist, but expect the majority of questions to test College Savings Plan rules.

sections.title: Key Tax Benefits
sections.content: 529 plans offer significant federal and state tax advantages for education savers.

sections.title: State Tax Treatment
sections.content:
Many states offer **state income tax deductions** for contributions:

• Deduction limited to in-state plans for some states
• Some states offer deduction for any plan
• Varies widely - know the client's state

sections.title: Contribution Rules
sections.content: There's no annual federal contribution limit for 529 plans, but several considerations apply:

sections.title: Contribution Considerations
rows: Contributions over $19,000/year (2026) per beneficiary use lifetime exclusion
NONE - anyone can contribute regardless of income

sections.title: 📊 Super-Funding (5-Year Election)
sections.content:
Contributors can make a lump sum contribution and spread it over 5 years for gift tax purposes.

**2026 Example:**
• Maximum super-fund amount: $19,000 × 5 = **$95,000**
• Married couple splitting gifts: $38,000 × 5 = **$190,000**

**Rules:**
• Must file Form 709 each year
• Contributor must survive 5 years or excess reverts to estate
• No additional gifts to that beneficiary during 5-year period

sections.title: SECURE 2.0 Act: 529 to Roth IRA Rollover
sections.content: **Effective 2024**: Unused 529 funds can be rolled to a **Roth IRA** for the beneficiary. This is a major planning opportunity for families with excess education savings.

sections.title: 529 to Roth IRA Rollover Requirements
rows: Subject to Roth IRA contribution limit ($7,500 in 2026)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: SECURE 2.0 Roth Rollover
sections.content: Remember the key numbers: **15 years** (account age), **$35K** (lifetime limit), **5-year lookback** (recent contributions excluded).

sections.title: Qualified Education Expenses
sections.content: Understanding what qualifies for tax-free 529 withdrawals is critical for exam success.

sections.title: K-12 Tuition (Limited)
sections.content:
• Up to **$10,000 per year** per beneficiary
• **Tuition only** - not books, supplies, or room

sections.title: Apprenticeship Programs
sections.content: Registered apprenticeship programs now qualify for fees, books, supplies, and equipment.

sections.title: Non-Qualified Distributions
sections.content: If funds are used for non-qualified expenses, different tax treatment applies:

sections.title: Penalty Waivers
sections.content:
The 10% penalty is waived if:

• Beneficiary receives tax-free scholarship (to extent of scholarship)
• Beneficiary attends U.S. military academy
• Beneficiary dies or becomes disabled
• Beneficiary receives qualifying employer education assistance

sections.title: Investment Options
rows: Automatically becomes more conservative as child ages

sections.title: ⚠️ Investment Change Limit
sections.content: Can change investment strategy **twice per year**. However, you can change anytime when **changing beneficiaries**.

sections.title: Account Ownership and Control
rows: Contributes, controls investments, requests distributions, can change beneficiary
Person whose education expenses are paid; no control rights

sections.title: Changing Beneficiaries
sections.content:
Can change to another **member of the family** without tax consequences:

• Siblings, children, parents, grandparents
• First cousins, aunts, uncles
• In-laws of the above
• Spouse of the beneficiary

sections.title: Financial Aid Impact (FAFSA Treatment)
rows: Previously distributions as untaxed income (now simplified)

sections.title: FAFSA Simplification Act (2024)
sections.content: Grandparent-owned 529s no longer count against student aid under the FAFSA Simplification Act.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: **529 College Savings Plans** = investment accounts with tax-free growth for education
**No federal deduction**, but many states offer deductions
**Super-funding**: Up to 5 years of gifts at once ($95,000 single/$190,000 married in 2026)
**SECURE 2.0**: Unused funds can roll to Roth IRA ($35,000 lifetime limit)
Non-qualified withdrawals: **Earnings taxed + 10% penalty**

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements regarding 529 plans is MOST accurate?
options: Earnings in a 529 plan are always subject to federal income tax upon withdrawal.
529 plans are only available for tuition expenses at four-year colleges and universities.
Contributions to a 529 plan are always deductible for federal income tax purposes.
529 plans offer tax-advantaged savings for qualified education expenses.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: 529 plans provide tax-advantaged savings, meaning earnings grow tax-deferred and withdrawals are tax-free when used for qualified education expenses. While some states offer deductions for contributions, this is not a federal benefit. 529 plans can be used for more than just four-year colleges, and earnings are only taxed if not used for qualified expenses.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the core benefit that makes 529 plans attractive for college savings.

reveal.question: Explain how the SECURE 2.0 Act impacts 529 plans, focusing on potential rollovers.
reveal.answer: The SECURE 2.0 Act allows beneficiaries of 529 plans to roll over unused funds into a Roth IRA, subject to certain conditions. Specifically, the 529 plan must have been open for more than 15 years, the rollover is subject to annual Roth IRA contribution limits and a lifetime cap, and the beneficiary of the Roth IRA must be the same as the 529 plan beneficiary.

sections.title: College Savings vs. Prepaid Tuition Plans

rows.label: Investment Risk
values: The account owner directs investments; performance depends on market conditions.

rows.label: Qualified Expenses
values: Can be used for tuition, fees, room and board, books, and supplies at eligible educational institutions (including K-12 up to $10,000 per year).

rows.label: Tuition Locking
values: Does not lock in tuition rates.

rows.label: State Residency
values: Often, but not always, open to out-of-state residents.

title: Coverdell ESAs and Education Savings Bonds
description: Compare Coverdell ESA contribution limits, income restrictions, and K-12 advantages with 529 plans and savings bond education exclusions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Beyond 529 plans, Coverdell ESAs and certain savings bonds offer additional tax-advantaged education savings options. Understanding the differences helps advisors recommend the right mix of education funding vehicles for each client situation.

sections.title: Coverdell Education Savings Accounts (ESAs)
sections.content: Coverdell ESAs (formerly Education IRAs) provide tax-free growth for education expenses with some key limitations.

sections.title: Coverdell ESA Overview
rows: Beneficiary must be under 18 (exceptions for special needs)
Funds must be used by age 30

sections.title: Key Advantage of Coverdell
sections.content: **Broader K-12 qualified expenses** - not just tuition, but also books and supplies, tutoring, uniforms, transportation, and computer equipment.

sections.title: Distribution Rules
sections.content: Coverdell ESAs offer different qualified expenses for higher education versus K-12.

sections.title: Higher Education Qualified Expenses
sections.content: Same as 529: tuition, fees, books, room & board, computer equipment.

sections.title: Non-Qualified Distributions
sections.content:
Same as 529:

• Contributions return tax-free
• Earnings taxed as ordinary income + **10% penalty**

sections.title: ⚠️ Coordination with 529
sections.content: Can contribute to **both** Coverdell and 529 in same year for same beneficiary, but total education tax benefits cannot exceed actual qualified expenses. Expenses used for Coverdell can't also be used for 529 or AOTC/LLC.

sections.title: Series EE and Series I Savings Bonds
sections.content: Interest from EE and I bonds can be **tax-free** if used for qualified higher education expenses.

sections.title: Education Tax Exclusion Requirements
rows: Owner must be **at least 24** at issuance
Owner or co-owner must be taxpayer, spouse, or dependent
Tuition and REQUIRED fees at eligible institution

sections.title: Bond Exclusion Calculation
sections.content:
If qualified expenses are less than total bond proceeds, a partial exclusion applies:

$$\text{Excludable Interest} = \text{Interest} \times \frac{\text{Qualified Expenses}}{\text{Bond Proceeds}}$$

sections.title: 📊 Bond Exclusion Example
sections.content:
**Bond redemption**: $15,000 (principal) + $3,000 (interest) = $18,000
**Qualified expenses**: $12,000

$$\text{Excludable Interest} = \$3,000 \times \frac{\$12,000}{\$18,000} = \$2,000$$

**Tax-free interest**: $2,000
**Taxable interest**: $1,000

sections.title: Coordination Rules
sections.content: Understanding how multiple education accounts interact is crucial for planning.

sections.title: Same Year Contributions
sections.content:
Can fund multiple accounts for same beneficiary:

• 529 Plan
• Coverdell ESA
• UTMA/UGMA
• Trust accounts

sections.title: ⚠️ No Double-Dipping
sections.content:
An expense can only qualify for **ONE** tax benefit:

• 529 tax-free distribution, OR
• Coverdell tax-free distribution, OR
• AOTC/LLC tax credit, OR
• Qualified scholarship

**Strategy**: Use some expenses for tax credits (AOTC/LLC) and others for 529/Coverdell.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: **Coverdell ESA**: $2,000/year limit, income phase-out, must use by age 30
**Coverdell K-12 advantage**: Broader expenses (books, tutoring) vs. 529 tuition-only limit
**Savings bonds education exclusion**: Owner must be 24+ at issuance
**Bonds must be in parent/taxpayer name**, not child's name
**Same expense cannot be used** for both 529/Coverdell AND education credits

knowledgeCheck.question:
Which of the following statements regarding Coverdell ESAs is CORRECT?

A) Contributions are tax-deductible.
B) The maximum annual contribution is $4,000 per beneficiary.
C) Contributions can be made regardless of the contributor's income.
D) The beneficiary must use the funds by age 30, unless they have special needs.
options: B) The maximum annual contribution is $4,000 per beneficiary.
C) Contributions can be made regardless of the contributor's income.
D) The beneficiary must use the funds by age 30, unless they have special needs.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is D. Coverdell ESAs require funds to be used by age 30 (unless the beneficiary is a special needs beneficiary). Contributions are NOT tax-deductible. The maximum annual contribution is $2,000 per beneficiary. Contributions are limited based on the contributor's income.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the contribution limit and the beneficiary age restriction.

sections.title: Tax Implications of Education Savings Bonds

reveal.question: Under what circumstances are Series EE or Series I bonds exempt from federal income tax when used for education expenses?
reveal.answer: The bonds must be redeemed in the same year that qualified education expenses are incurred. The bonds must be registered in the name of the parent (if the child is a dependent) and the taxpayer's modified adjusted gross income (MAGI) must be below a certain level (which is subject to change each year). The taxpayer must be over age 24 when the bond is issued.

rows.label: Contribution Limit
values: $2,000 per beneficiary per year
Varies by state; often much higher

rows.label: Eligible Education Expenses
values: K-12 and higher education
Primarily higher education; some plans allow K-12

rows.label: Control of Investments
values: Greater control over investments
Limited investment options, typically managed by the state or institution

title: Education Tax Credits and Deductions
description: Compare AOTC and Lifetime Learning Credit, apply income phase-outs, and coordinate education credits with 529/Coverdell distributions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Education tax credits provide dollar-for-dollar reductions in tax liability, often more valuable than deductions. Understanding when to use AOTC vs. LLC and how to coordinate with 529 plans can save families thousands of dollars.

sections.title: American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC)
sections.content: The **most valuable** education tax credit for undergraduates.

content.definition: Books, supplies, equipment required for courses

sections.title: Lifetime Learning Credit (LLC)
sections.content: Broader eligibility than AOTC, but smaller benefit.

sections.title: LLC Overview
rows: **Unlimited** - any year, any number of years

sections.title: LLC Qualified Expenses
sections.content:
• Tuition and required fees
• Course materials if required to be purchased from institution
• **NOT** included: Books purchased elsewhere, room and board

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: AOTC vs. LLC
sections.content:
**AOTC = American = First 4 is the OPPORTUNITY**
**LLC = Lifetime = LEARNING for LIFE (unlimited years)**

Use AOTC for first 4 years of college; switch to LLC for 5th year or graduate school.

sections.title: Choosing the Right Credit
sections.content:
**Choose AOTC when:**
• First 4 years of undergraduate
• The student has no felony drug conviction
• Need refundable credit (low tax liability)

**Choose LLC when:**
• Graduate school or professional degree
• 5th year of undergraduate
• Less than half-time enrollment
• Taking courses for job skills (not degree)

sections.title: Student Loan Interest Deduction
sections.content: **Above-the-line deduction** (reduces AGI regardless of itemizing).

sections.title: Student Loan Interest Deduction Overview
rows: Cannot claim if claimed as dependent

sections.title: Qualified Loans
sections.content:
**Qualified:**
• Federal student loans
• Private student loans
• Loans from any lender for qualified education expenses

**NOT Qualified:**
• Loans from relatives
• Employer plan loans
• Loans from qualified retirement plans

sections.title: ⚠️ Coordination with 529/Coverdell
sections.content: **You cannot double-dip**: Expenses used for tax-free distributions CANNOT also be used for education credits.

sections.title: 📊 Optimal Strategy Example
sections.content:
For a student with $15,000 in tuition and $5,000 in books:

**Option A: All 529**
• Withdraw $20,000 tax-free from 529
• No education credits

**Option B: Split (Usually Better)**
• Withdraw $16,000 from 529 for room/board and remaining tuition
• Pay $4,000 of tuition out-of-pocket (or taxable 529)
• Claim AOTC: 100% × $2,000 + 25% × $2,000 = **$2,500 credit**

**Net Benefit**: $2,500 credit > tax avoided on $4,000 of 529 earnings

sections.title: Employer-Provided Education Assistance (Section 127)
rows: Any education (doesn't need to be job-related)
Can be used for student loan repayment (through 2025)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: **AOTC**: $2,500/student, 40% refundable, first 4 undergraduate years only
**LLC**: $2,000/return, non-refundable, unlimited years, includes graduate school
**Student loan interest**: Up to $2,500 deduction, above-the-line
**No double-dipping**: Separate expenses for 529/Coverdell vs. credits
**Employer education assistance**: $5,250 tax-free per year

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following expenses qualifies for the American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC)?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Tuition and required fees are specifically listed as qualified expenses for the AOTC. Room and board, transportation, and medical expenses are not.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the specific items that are included versus excluded.

reveal.question: Why is the American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC) often considered the most valuable education tax credit for undergraduates?
reveal.answer: The AOTC is often the most valuable because it offers a larger maximum credit amount, a portion of it is refundable (meaning you can receive some of the credit back as a refund even if you don't owe any taxes), and it is available for the first four years of higher education.

rows.label: Student Level
values: Undergraduate (first 4 years)
Undergraduate, Graduate, Professional

rows.label: Credit Amount
values: Up to $2,500
Up to $2,000

rows.label: Refundable
values: 40% refundable (up to $1,000)
Nonrefundable

rows.label: Coursework
values: Degree seeking
Degree seeking or courses to improve job skills

title: Financial Aid and Student Loan Strategies
description: Understand FAFSA methodology, asset treatment differences, federal vs. private loans, and strategies to maximize financial aid eligibility

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Understanding financial aid can help families maximize eligibility and minimize education costs. The difference between parent-owned and student-owned assets can mean thousands of dollars in aid eligibility.

sections.title: FAFSA Overview
sections.content:
The **Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA)** determines eligibility for:

• Federal grants (Pell Grant, FSEOG)
• Federal student loans (Direct)
• Work-study programs
• Many state and institutional aid programs

sections.title: FAFSA Simplification Act (Effective 2024-25)
sections.content:
Major changes include:

• New **Student Aid Index (SAI)** replaces EFC
• Reduced questions (108 → 36)
• Simplified needs test for more families
• Grandparent-owned 529s no longer counted as student income

sections.title: Student Aid Index (SAI) Calculation
sections.content: The SAI formula considers both parent and student income and assets.

sections.title: Parent Asset Assessment
rows: Based on age ($0 for younger parents)

sections.title: ⚠️ Critical Asset Distinction
sections.content: Parent assets are assessed at a maximum of **5.64%** while student assets are assessed at **20%**. This 3.5× difference makes asset ownership structure critically important.

sections.title: Asset Treatment by Account Type
sections.content: Different accounts have very different financial aid impacts.

sections.title: Parental Assets (5.64% Maximum)
sections.content:
• **Parent-owned 529 plans** (student is beneficiary)
• **Cash, checking, savings**
• **Investment accounts** (non-retirement)
• **Real estate** (excluding primary home)
• **Business equity** (if >100 employees)

sections.title: Student Assets (20%)
sections.content:
• **Student-owned 529 plans**
• **UTMA/UGMA custodial accounts** (student is owner at majority)
• **Student savings/checking**
• **Student investments**

sections.title: Excluded Assets
sections.content:
• **Retirement accounts** (401k, IRA, Roth IRA, pensions)
• **Primary residence equity**
• **Cash value of life insurance**
• **Personal property** (cars, furniture)
• **Small business equity** (<100 employees, family-owned)

sections.title: 📊 Impact Analysis Example
sections.content:
**Client Situation:**
• Parent-owned 529: $100,000
• UTMA in student's name: $50,000

**Financial Aid Impact:**
• 529 impact: $100,000 × 5.64% = **$5,640** expected contribution
• UTMA impact: $50,000 × 20% = **$10,000** expected contribution

**Key Insight:** UTMA nearly **doubles** the financial aid impact despite being half the value!

sections.title: Federal Student Loan Types
sections.content: Understanding the different federal loan options helps families make informed borrowing decisions.

sections.title: Parent PLUS Loans
rows: Parent of dependent student, no adverse credit
Up to cost of attendance minus other aid

sections.title: Financial Aid Strategies
sections.content: Several legitimate strategies can help maximize financial aid eligibility.

sections.title: 1. Asset Positioning
sections.content:
**Before FAFSA filing:**

• Reduce cash in student's name
• Pay down debt with liquid assets
• Max out parent retirement contributions (excluded)
• Consider prepaying non-education expenses

sections.title: 2. Timing Income
sections.content:
• Defer bonuses if possible
• Realize capital gains in non-FAFSA years
• Control income recognition where possible

sections.title: 3. Use Parent-Owned 529s
sections.content:
• Counts as parent asset (5.64%) not student (20%)
• Qualified distributions don't count as income

sections.title: 4. FAFSA Filing Timing
sections.content:
• File early (October 1 for following fall)
• Use prior-prior year tax data
• Submit corrections promptly if situation changes

sections.title: Private Student Loans
sections.content: Private loans should typically be a last resort after exhausting federal options.

sections.title: When to Consider Private Loans
sections.content:
• After exhausting federal options
• If federal limits don't meet needs
• If credit-worthy with low rates available

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: **Parent assets**: 5.64% max assessment; **Student assets**: 20% assessment
**UTMA disadvantage**: Counted as student asset (4× worse for aid)
**Retirement accounts**: Excluded from FAFSA
**FAFSA Simplification**: Grandparent 529s no longer hurt aid
**Federal loans first**: Better protections than private loans

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is MOST accurate regarding the Student Aid Index (SAI)?
options: The SAI is solely determined by the student's income and assets.
The SAI is solely determined by the parents' income and assets.
The SAI is a measure of a family's ability to contribute to college costs, considering both parent and student income and assets.
The SAI is only used to determine eligibility for federal student loans.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Student Aid Index (SAI) considers both the parent's and the student's income and assets to determine the family's ability to contribute to college costs. It's a comprehensive assessment, not limited to just one party or type of aid.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about who is primarily responsible for funding a student's education.

sections.title: Federal Aid Programs - Name That Program!

reveal.question: Which federal grant program provides aid to undergraduate students with exceptional financial need, and unlike a loan, does not need to be repaid?
reveal.answer: The Pell Grant. It's a need-based federal grant that doesn't require repayment, making it a valuable resource for eligible students.

rows.label: Interest Rates
values: Often fixed and may be lower
Can be fixed or variable and are often higher

rows.label: Repayment Options
values: Offer income-driven repayment plans and deferment/forbearance options
Typically have fewer repayment options and less flexible deferment/forbearance policies

rows.label: Eligibility
values: Available to eligible students who complete the FAFSA
Credit-based; requires a credit check and may require a co-signer
